title: User-controlled density vs settings management vs data grid vs saved view
summary: Use user-controlled density when users choose how compact a surface appears; settings management when users need a durable configuration hub; data grid when rows and columns need managed interaction; and saved view when users save filters, columns, sorting, or grouping as a reusable data view.
decisionRules: Choose user-controlled density when the user needs to switch between comfortable, standard, compact, or spacious spacing while keeping the same content, order, filters, and task model.
Choose settings management when density is only one row inside a broader durable configuration page with categories, scopes, dependencies, save states, and reset behavior.
Choose data grid when the main issue is row-and-column interaction such as managed focus, cell editing, selection, sorting, filtering, virtualization, or keyboard navigation.
Choose saved view when users need to persist columns, filters, sorting, grouping, visible fields, or data scope as a named reusable view rather than only changing spacing.
Density controls must state scope, such as this table, this workspace, this device, or this account, before the user applies or saves the compactness choice.
Density changes should preserve focus, scroll position, selected rows, open detail, edited values, and current filters while row height and control spacing update.
Do not use compact density to hide required labels, status, validation, row actions, touch targets, or accessibility affordances.
Use responsive layout changes when the problem is screen width or breakpoint behavior; use density when the same viewport can support different information compactness preferences.
Use zoom or text-size controls when the user needs larger text or accessibility scaling; density must not override browser zoom, OS text size, or minimum target requirements.
Let users preview, reset to system default, and persist density only within the named scope; never silently change a shared team's view from a personal compactness choice.
failureModes: A compact mode shrinks text, checkboxes, menus, and row actions until labels truncate and touch targets become unreliable.
Density changes reorder columns, clear filters, lose row selection, or discard unsaved cell edits.
The product stores a personal compact setting as the shared workspace default without warning.
A saved view is used to represent density, so changing spacing also changes columns and filters.
A data grid exposes a density menu but row height, header height, batch actions, and detail rows no longer align.
Mobile density copies the desktop compact table and forces horizontal scrolling instead of preserving readable row identity.

sources.note: Material documents coordinated density settings and the tradeoff between compactness and usability.
MUI documents standard, compact, and comfortable density values and user override behavior for data grids.
Carbon documents data-table row sizing and matching header and row sizes.
Cloudscape documents comfortable and compact density modes based on spacing tokens.
